text: High-Resolution X-ray Computed Tomography (HRXCT) or micro-CT (µCT) is one of the most popular non-destructive 3D imaging and analysis methods for investigating the internal structure of wide range of objects from sediments to artefacts and even fossils including geomaterials. While the application of X-ray micro-CT is rapidly increasing in earth science research, the demands on this technique are reaching certain physical and/or practical limits, and therefore there is ample scope for further research into the various technical problems that are emerging when the performance of the technique is pushed to the limits. In this review paper, we summarize the principle, advantages and limitations of X-ray CT and provide an overview of its current applications in the geosciences. The main advantages of this technique are that X-ray micro-CT is a non-destructive characterization technique that allows 4D imaging of the internal structural evolution at the micrometer-scale down to a few hundred nanometers. Some of the disadvantages of this technique include operator dependency in image analysis and discretization effects and possible artefacts. However, the progress in technical and computational aspects are continually improving the applicability of this technique in geosciences, which is indicated by the rapidly growing number of publications in this field. This trend is expected to continue and therefore the micro-CT is expected to become one of the important tools in geosciences.
